issue_title: Vertical and horizontal scrollbar not both visible in toggled output
issue_body:
When displaying a large pandas DataFrame, you get some scrollbars. And starting from pandas 0.16.2, we leave the overflowing fully to the notebook itself (we removed `style="max-width:1500px;overflow:auto;"` from the html output, see https://github.com/pydata/pandas/pull/10232).

But I noticed another issue: when having a wide frame that does not fit the notebook width, you get horizontal scrollbar at the bottom of the output. When you also use the 'scroll output' feature, you get a vertical scrollbar as well. But the horizontal is only visible when you first scroll down to the bottom. To illustrate:

![nb2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1020496/9037638/97ebdac0-39ed-11e5-8a04-fae8927bded9.png)

![nb1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1020496/9037639/9a86b5fc-39ed-11e5-89c5-57865b47760f.png)

So if you want to see all columns of the first rows, you first have to scroll down, to be able to scroll right, then scroll up again to see the rows.

I suppose the one scrollbar belongs to the output itself, and the other to the toggled ouput area, but would it be possible to have them both on the toggled output area?

I'm trying to embed a snakeviz plot into a notebook. My notebook profiles a chunk of code, generates a pstats table, and passes that to snakeviz. This appears to be working as expected when I dump the generated html to a static file and then load that separately into a browser. 
However, when attempting to embed the html into the current notebook, it generates these javascript errors:

Uncaught TypeError: $(...).dataTable is not a function
profiler.ipynb:4 Uncaught ReferenceError: Immutable is not defined
profiler.ipynb:104 Uncaught ReferenceError: sv_call_stack is not defined
profiler.ipynb:17 Uncaught ReferenceError: sv_root_func_name is not defined

The notebook console output shows all the relevant javascripts being loaded, though the above error suggests the javascript runtime doesn't quite contain the expected objects.

As it's unfortunately not possible to attach tarballs to bug reports I have checked in the notebook into my repo fork at https://github.com/stefanseefeld/notebook/tree/bug (into the 'testcase' subdirectory in the 'bug' branch.

I'm currently running a notebook server on a folder with 35 notebooks. I usually have more than one notebook open and running and usually these notebooks are not next to each other.

This leads to some bad things when I want to shutdown some notebooks:

* When I mark notebooks and shutdown the notebooks, the notebooks are still marked. Sometimes it happens that I shutdown notebooks which were selected further down (not visible in the currently visible screen)
* When I want to shutdown a notebook further down in the list, I first have to scroll up to deselect all, then scroll down to mark the notebook, then up again to shutdown.

I remember that the notebook had buttons next to the names, where I could shutdown a single notebook. I would really like to get these buttons back.

issue_title: Clarify inspect_reply fields in messaging spec
issue_body:
In the `inspect_request` and `inspect_reply` [documentation](https://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/messaging.html#introspection) there are 3 fields: `found`, `data`, `metadata`.

It is not 100% clear whether, on an error, it is required for the `found`, `data`, and `metadata` keys to be absent, or whether it is expected that they exist and are `false`, empty, and empty, respectively. 

I would suggest clarifying that those fields should exist *only* if `status` is `ok` (or at least clarify that those fields are only required if `status` is `ok`). (Or, if there is a reason that those fields should exist when the status is `err`, clarify that those fields *must* exist in all cases, as that would be somewhat unintuitive)

(As with the [other](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1575) [issues](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/1574) I filed recently, this isn't necessarily anything serious or urgent, but having a strict spec will be really helpful for developers outside the core notebook and jupyter project in the long term! :) )

    http://ipython.org/ipython-doc/dev/install/install.html#installnotebook

which was a lot less than helpful, which I found by following the link at the top of the Jupyter docs:

    http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.org/en/latest/notebook.html#introduction

Seems like making INSTALL.md at the root of `jupyter/notebook` and linking to it from the homepage, or making a jupyter specific installation section in the `jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.org` would both be cool until a super smooth install process has been figured out...

Apologies if I missed an obvious link somewhere...

Some files, e.g. `.html` files, can logically be both viewed (via `/files/`) and edited (via `/edit/`). Currently, we have just the one link that goes to `/edit/` or `/files/`, depending on the file type. We don't currently have separate links to these to URLs for files, but we probably should.

One option is to add view, edit to the list of actions along with delete, rename. Another is to add the links inline, perhaps on hover.

JupyterLab doesn't have this issue since it has an Open With... dialog, so if we take much longer to get around to this one, pointing to JupyterLab may end up being the solution.

I know this has been discussed a few places, but I can't find a dedicated issue for it, so opening here. If someone finds a duplicate, we can close this one.

cc @dsblank for interest, @ellisonbg for UI

Currently, in chrome, if I want to recall the previous command in the terminal via `Ctrl+N`, I just get a new window. Also, in a terminal vim setup, I cannot use `Ctrl+W` to move cursor between windows because that would just close the chrome tab.

I'd appreciate if there's an option to disable the native browser shortcuts when the terminal is open.

issue_title: Update messages spinner and close button to work without hard dependency on font-awesome

issue_body:
Let's say we have the following model;

```python
class Foo(Model):
    bar = BooleanType()
```

And then we create an instance of it;

```python
a = Foo(dict(bar='a'), validate=False)
```

This raises `DataError` (obviously because `bar` is not a bool) however I'd rather that DataError is not raised until I specically call `a.validate()`.

Are there any workarounds for this or is this something that is planned to be rectified?

The reason why this is important to me is because I want all errors to be reported to the user, not just some of them, only for them to resubmit their request to find out there are more errors. For example;

```python
class Foo(Model):
    bar = BooleanType()
    eggs = StringType(choices=['yes', 'no', 'maybe'])
```

1. User submits a form with `bar='a'` and `eggs='adsf'`
2. User is notified of an error about `bar` not being valid.
3. User fixes `bar` and resubmits form
4. User is notified for more errors for `eggs`

From the users point of view that is extremely frustrating and from a programmers point of view even more frustrating.

Obviously this is a pretty trivial example and it's unlikely given the datatypes you would give the user a freeform text field, however you get my point.

I've run into an issue with large matrices and memory. There seem to be two problems:

1) Memory isn't being released on successive calls of grad. e.g.

```
a = 10000
b = 10000
A = np.random.randn(a)
B = np.random.randn(b)

def fn(x):
    M = A[:, na] + x[na, :]
    return M[0, 0]
    
g = grad(fn)

for i in range(100):
    g(B)
```
is ramping up memory on each iteration.

2) Memory isn't being released during the backwards pass e.g.

```
k = 10
def fn(x):
    res = 0
    for i in range(k):
        res = res + np.sum(x)
    return res
g = grad(fn)
b = 200000
g(np.random.randn(b))
```
This seems to scale in memory (for each call) as O(k), which don't think is the desired behaviour. For b=150000 this effect does not happen, however.
Could the next minor release be created? It would make simplify the tool installation process for the Galaxy Standalone VM currently being worked on.

issue_title: Dies too soon with cmake missing
issue_body:
skbuild fails to import if cmake is missing. This means that the following code will not work (untested, so it might not work as is, too):

```python
import subprocess

# Add CMake as a build requirement if cmake is not installed or is too low a version
setup_requires = []
try:
    cmake_version = subprocess.check_output(["cmake", "--version"])
    cmake_version = [int(x) for x in cmake_version.splitlines()[0].split()[-1].split('.')]
    if cmake_version < (3,4):
        setup_requires.append('cmake')
except OSError:
    setup_requires.append('cmake')
```

This forces the addition of `cmake` to the `pyproject.toml`, which could install an older version of cmake (3.10 instead of 3.11), and could affect the user (cmake from python might be installed in the path). If scikit build didn't fail until setup was run (and `setup_requires` was run), then the code at the beginning would be less invasive.

Note: testing code like this is easy with:

```bash
docker run -it ubuntu
apt update && apt install g++ python-pip python-dev git ninja-build
pip install pip==10.0b1
# get a scikit-build project and cd to it
python -m pip install .
```

Due to what I consider a bug in pip 10, you have to use `python -m pip` instead of `pip`.

issue_title: ssl profiles and parent profile inheritance
issue_body:
I've found what I believe to be a limitation to the current ssl profile implementation.  When I run update on a profile, it sets the custom flag to enable on all attributes, breaking the inheritance from the parent profile on options I did not intend to modify.

Example, take an existing ssl profile, which has the following configuration:

```
ltm profile client-ssl /Common/test {
    app-service none
    cert /Common/mycert.crt
    cert-key-chain {
        mycert_ca-bundle {
            cert /Common/mycert.crt
            chain /Common/ca-bundle.crt
            key /Common/mycert.key
        }
    }
    chain /Common/ca-bundle.crt
    defaults-from /Common/clientssl
    inherit-certkeychain false
    key /Common/mycert.key
    passphrase none
```

Now perform an update via the SDK

    profile = mr.tm.ltm.profile.client_ssls.client_ssl.load(name='test', partition='Common')
    profile.secureRenegotiation = 'require-strict'
    profile.update()


The result will be that all options are 'checked' in the WebUI, and the resulting config now looks like this:

```
ltm profile client-ssl /Common/test {
    alert-timeout indefinite
    allow-dynamic-record-sizing disabled
    allow-expired-crl disabled
    allow-non-ssl disabled
    app-service none
    authenticate once
    authenticate-depth 9
    ca-file none
    cache-size 262144
    cache-timeout 3600
    .... shortened, you get the idea ...
```

I understand the REST interface doesn't have the inherited flag like SOAP, but you can just PUT the option you want to modify leaving the other options as is.  the update method apparently does a PUT on every option(having no reference to what was inherited and what was not), and thus removes the inherited flag.  This will really hamper the ability to use the update method and preserve parent profile inheritance.

Is there some other way to do this without breaking the inheritance on options I do not want to modify and need to continue to inherit from the parent profile?

Thanks

issue_title: Anonymous order verification hash uses insecure md5
issue_body:
In `src/oscar/apps/order/abstract_models.py`, the `AbstractOrder` model has a `verification_hash` method, which uses the insecure MD5 hashing algorithm to compute a verification code.

I Am Not A Cryptographer, but my spidey sense is tingling...

- `key = '%s%s' % (self.number, settings.SECRET_KEY)`  <- user already knows the number; hash input is a simple concatenation of number and secret key
- `hash = hashlib.md5(key.encode('utf8'))` <- hash is also known to the user

Wouldn't that make the site-wide secret key recoverable from an anonymous order URL?

issue_title: think referencing seems to be broken in 1.1 but is ok in 1.0 (FileNotFoundException)
issue_body:
Hi,

I assume this could be a bug as i have been dealing with this all day after trying to reference different libraries. After further research i found out this does not seem to be an issue in 1.0 but only is affecting me on 1.1. I have been testing this on a web application using .Net core but others seem to be having the same issue even on the application versions.

I am using Visual Studio 2017, i have managed to replicated this problem on 3 machines now,  you can replicated the issue very easily by doing the following steps:-

- make a new asp.net core web application (.Net core) and a class library (.Net core) (**both .NETCoreApp 1.1 for target frameworks**). 
- build the empty library
- go to the web application, add the reference and reference the namespace in your HomeController.cs
- initialise the empty class from the library in the Index function.
- hit run

it will build and run the project but it will fail to load the library when it hits the function you have referenced the code in. It gives a "FileNotFoundException: Could not load file or assembly" error

i have also found some StackOverflow threads that seem to be related to the same issue as well.
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43662331/how-do-i-add-a-net-core-class-library-dll-to-asp-net-core-project/43913795#43913795

issue_title: Standalone Renewal: Automation and Domain Verification
issue_body:
The renewal process becomes cumbersome, when I have to restart or manually verify the domain.  Especially, when using letsencrypt certs on an unsupported platform.  For instance, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

Please consider a method to, renew by authenticating against a key or previous validation.  Such as ```privkey.pem``` or other variable.  **Maybe?**

I understand the reason for verification, no looking to circumvent it.  There should me a method to verify once and allow for a simpler re-verification.  How about, allowing the manual method ```.hidden_path_name/file``` to remain valid (unless a single check fails).

Otherwise, for me, renewal involve DNS changes to point to a temporary server.  Renewal, push certs to live system, then revert the DNS.

Anyway, just some thoughts on enhancing the renewal process.  _Thanks for reading._

issue_title: Fails to Parse Apache Configuration File on Host with Multiple Domains managed by Plesk
issue_body:
I've used letsencrypt successfully on other servers, however when I test it on a server that is hosting multiple domains it fails to correctly parse the apache config file. When I attempt to run it I get the following error: 
The apache plugin is not working; there may be problems with your existing configuration.
The error was: PluginError(('There has been an error in parsing the file (%s): %s', u'/etc/httpd/conf/plesk.conf.d/server.conf', u'Syntax error'),)
However when I check the config file for errors, it returns an ok status: 
apachectl configtest
Syntax OK
Below you can see the configuration file: https://gist.github.com/sokser/b0618d033f75e5687983dfa1003a935f

issue_title: Make a default renewal script in /etc/letsencrypt/
issue_body: Perhaps it should be `/etc/letsencrypt/renew-certs`?  It can call the new `letsencrypt-renew` command, but is also a good place for pre and post renewal homework.

issue_title: Donate prompt gets printed for each renewed cert
issue_body: the donate prompt needs to check for the renew verb and not print if it's been specified

issue_title: debian/rules's override_dh_auto_test target is not idempotent
issue_body:
Running 'debian/rules binary' or any other target that pulls in override_dh_auto_test will fail every time after the first due to this command:
  ln -s $(CURDIR)/acme/acme/testdata $(CURDIR)/debian/python-acme/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/acme/
Adding
  rm -f $(CURDIR)/debian/python-acme/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/acme/testdata
before it would help.

issue_title: document threat/security model and supported ways of using
issue_body:
there is a more-or-less unrelated and out-of-scope security disussion in PR #200 which mostly comes from a unclear / undocumented threat model, still existing general problems in letsencrypt (like always and early requiring root, see also issue #204), scope of what a user can configure (or not), what a user can give (or not) on cli interface.

also it seems to be that usage of letsencrypt is meant not to be limited to direct manual commandline use *by some user*, but also as a tool that is embedded into other tools (like a library or via calling letsencrypt automatically).

to keep the discussion of PR #200 in-scope, I suggest to create documentation that gives threat models we need to deal with, documents already existing solutions against these threats or proposes yet-to-get implemented solutions (and shows how they are effective against the threat in case that is non-obvious).
for stuff that is still to do in general / global for misc. parts of the code, separate issues shall get opened.

issue_title: rrule does not play well with pytz tzinfo at daylight savings?
issue_body:
Hi again, it's not clear if this is a dateutil bug, but it's at least a problem for users:

I parse a calendar with the `icalendar` package which returns datetimes localized with `pytz`.

Problem:
When using rrule to expand a recurring event, the occurences do not seem to respect Daylight savings. Here's a stand alone example:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta,timezone
from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr
from pytz import timezone as pytz_tz
from dateutil import tz as du_tz
import dateutil.parser as dp

# taken from a calendar exported by FastMail
RRULE='FREQ=WEEKLY;BYDAY=TU,TH;UNTIL=20180317T065959Z'
DTSTART='20180116T130000'

# localize datetime with pytz (like icalendar package)
zone = pytz_tz('America/Los_Angeles')
start = dp.parse(DTSTART)
start = zone.localize(start)

r=rrulestr(RRULE, dtstart=start)
n=datetime(year=2018, month=3, day=7,tzinfo=timezone.utc)
for d in r.between(n,n+timedelta(days=7)):
	print(d, d.tzinfo)
```

Output
```
2018-03-08 13:00:00-08:00 America/Los_Angeles
2018-03-13 13:00:00-08:00 America/Los_Angeles
```

But in America/Los_Angeles Daylight savings started on March 11, so I expect offset -07:00 on March 13.

If I convert the tzinfo of the start date to a dateutil timezone, by:

```python
# fix: replace tzinfo with corresponding dateutil tz
du_zone = du_tz.gettz(str(zone))
start=start.replace(tzinfo=du_zone)
```

The output is
```
2018-03-08 13:00:00-08:00 tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/America/Los_Angeles')
2018-03-13 13:00:00-07:00 tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/America/Los_Angeles')
```

But, for reference, with `zone = pytz_tz('America/Los_Angeles')` I get
```python
>>> str(zone.localize(datetime.datetime(2018,3,8,13)))
'2018-03-08 13:00:00-08:00'
>>> str(zone.localize(datetime.datetime(2018,3,13,13)))
'2018-03-13 13:00:00-07:00'
``` 
I cannot figure out at what point this information is lost.

Again, it is unclear to me if this is an error of pytz or dateutil, but either way it is likely of interest to the community. Thanks!

Migrating issue from launchpad [issue #727525](https://bugs.launchpad.net/dateutil/+bug/727525).

You can read the full exchange there, but the gist is that while there's a keyword for "weeks" in relativedelta, you cannot retrieve the number of weeks in a relativedelta object as you can for the other time periods.

The solution would be to offer a `weeks` property that returns `self.days // 7`. Seems reasonable.

This has not happened smaller but othewise similar files uploaded from the same machine

issue_body:
Hi,

I'm using the beta to create object detectors using my late 2015 iMac (graphics: AMD Radeon R9 M380 2048 MB). The performance has improved significantly over the use of my computer's CPU but it's still not good enough. 

I'm thinking of getting an external GPU to help speed things up. I've heard that eGPUs have better support in macOS Mojave. If I am to get a supported eGPU (say a Vega 64) and connect it to my mac, would the beta of turi create automatically recognize it as my computer's GPU and start using it to train the neural network, or will I need to take additional measures to get the beta to incorporate the GPU into the training process?

If I need to take extra measures, what might those be?

also, can I get Turi create beta to use both my computer's GPU and the external GPU at the same time?

thanks

issue_title: Inconsistent groupby aggregation between SUM and MEAN
issue_body:
Summing over None values in an SArray returns 0.0.
I think we would expect aggregation on SUM to return None instead of 0.

Doing the same with MEAN returns None, as expected.
```
import turicreate as tc
import turicreate.aggregate as agg
sf= tc.SFrame({'c1':['a'], 'c2':[None]})
```

`_sf1.groupby('c1', agg.SUM('c2'))`
returns 
Data:
+----+-----------+
_| c1 | Sum of c2 |_
+----+-----------+
| b  |    0.0    |
+----+-----------+
[1 rows x 2 columns]

while `sf1.groupby('c1', agg.MEAN('c2'))`
returns 
_Data:_
+----+-----------+
| c1 | Avg of c2 |
+----+-----------+
| b  |    None   |
+----+-----------+
_[1 rows x 2 columns]_

issue_title: Loss and gradient is not correct when criterion's sizeAverage is set to false
issue_body:
When sizeAverage is false, the loss should be the sum of the losses of a whole batch, but in BigDL the sum of losses is calculated on each sub-batch on each executor and averaged over the cluster. 

That is over loss is larger than other framework by a factor of executor number.

The gradients has a similar issue.

We use latest git head 6602a8f6e8d7c550b363501e83fc20a28b8f6342 to do inception V1 and Resnet training, and find that training doesn't converge randomly:

Hi! We are working on realization of RL algorithms with BigDL and we need a few improvements.

1) Could you please make possible switching off report while optimizer creation. Since during RL I do model training many times, those reports just flood report window.

issue_title: Feature: Ability to refresh backend libraries
issue_body:
At the moment, there is no way (that I know of at least) to invoke `backend.LibraryProvider.refresh()` from within the UI.

This would be a nice feature to add to allow backends to be refreshed when the underlying library changes as many backends appear to be employing some sort of library caching mechanism.

Mopidy-Mobile implemented this using a `drag down` swipe, but we could also just re-use the refresh button approach that came out of https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/issues/107.

The progress timer can sometimes become stuck when playback of one track stops and playback of a new track begins. When that happens, the timer shows '(sync)' and the slider does not move. The problem is more likely to occur when switching from a long duration track to a shorter one.

This is related to how the web client currently makes use of [media-progress-timer](https://github.com/adamcik/media-progress-timer) (also see: https://github.com/adamcik/media-progress-timer/issues/3). Essentially, when the web client polls Mopidy for the time position it sometimes tries to apply the position of the previous track to the timer of the next track.

Possible solutions may be to either:

- rework https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/blob/34e2abf6129fce36547d58e484d875e2b874591a/mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/progress_timer.js#L98-L103 so that we never try to ``set`` the progress timer beyond its current ``duration`` (not sure how to do this as it is unclear exactly when we can expect all of the Mopidy events and track attributes to be in sync when a track change is busy occurring); or
- patch ``timer.js`` so that it does not automatically terminate the update loop when it reaches or exceeds ``duration``. 

I've tried a few changes to resolve this, but even with the most recent attempt (https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/commit/0965f0083e013a11670990f31339f56db366eba1) this is still an issue.

Dear all, 

Hello, I am fairly new to Z3 smt-solver, and I would like to get your opinions on the below problem.

So I have below code (main.py and func.py),

In main.py,
=======================================
from z3 import *
from func import *

# here, let's say i want to insist using main(), calculation_without_input1(), calculation_without_input2() for some reason

def main():
	calculation_without_input1() 
	calculation_without_input2()

global x, y, z, result1, result2

x, y, z, result1, result2 = Ints('x y z result1 result2')

main()

s = Solver()
s.add(x >= 0)
s.add(y >= 0)
s.add(z >= 0)
s.add(result2 == 100)

print(s.check())
result = s.model()
print(result)
=======================================

In func.py
=======================================
def calculation_without_input1():
	global x, y, z, result1, result2
	
	result1 = x^2 + y^2
	
	
def calculation_without_input2():
	global x, y, z, result1, result2
	
	result2 = result1 + z^2
=======================================


But, if I run main.py, I get error such as:
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 14, in <module>
    main()
  File "main.py", line 7, in main
    calculation_without_input1() 
  File "/home/relab-01/download/z3-master/build/python/func.py", line 5, in calculation_without_input1
    result1 = x^2 + y^2
NameError: global name 'x' is not defined
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

So my question is,

-> is there any way to define Z3 variables (variables x, y, z, result1, result2 in main.py file) so that I can use those values in other functions in other file?
(Here, let's say I want to insist on using those functions and separate files)

I am very sorry about the newbie question.

Thank you very much in advance :)

Best,
Lee.

Is possible to using Z3 for other function than + or - ?
for example Sin() cos() floor abs() etc.

is possible to generating 'word' similar in title?

As discussed [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1101#issuecomment-310680676) and [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1101#issuecomment-310686584)

Currently when we have an optimization problem Z3 prints `sat` followed by `objectives`. It would be nicer to stick to one s-expr per response rule that SMTLib2 adheres to. Adding a new command `(get-objectives)` as suggested by @wintersteiger might be a better choice.

Note that this would also unify the behavior of `lexicographic`, `boxed` and `pareto` modes: The first two do print objectives, but the last one doesn't. By implementing `(get-objectives)` as a separate command, Z3 can properly respond to the first two and say something appropriate for the `pareto` case. Right now, the client software has to manage that explicitly.

issue_title: Bug in maximize with forall constraints
issue_body:
Hello Z3 developers,

I was trying to find optimal bounds for a loop index, eg

for (i=5; i>=1; i--) { ... }

Minimal bug case is just the lower bound:

```
(declare-const x_min Int)
(assert (forall ((x Int)) (implies (<= 1 x) (<= x_min (- x 1)))))
(assert (<= x_min 5))
(maximize x_min)
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

Z3 4.6 returns 
```
sat
(model (define-fun x_min () Int 5))
```
which is wrong: x isn't bounded by 5.  However, changing the 1 to a 0, or any non-positive value I've tested:
```
(declare-const x_min Int)
(assert (forall ((x Int)) (implies (<= 0 x) (<= x_min (- x 1)))))
(assert (<= x_min 5))
(maximize x_min)
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
This returns:
```
sat
(model (define-fun x_min () Int (- 1)))
```
which is correct.

Also, is this sort of case supposed to be supported, or should I expect it to fail / take exponential time in general?

issue_title: Allow returning an empty string as a valid response
update_collection() does not work for models with unique constraints
issue_body:
Child objects that have a unique constraint on a non-PK field cannot be replaced with another object with the same value in the unique field. The add() and edit() methods cause the collection to be flushed before the records that aren't in the new data list are removed resulting in an uncaught integrity exception.

To fix this issue, I think we would need do the remove step first before adding and editing the remaining records. It may also be a good idea to disable flushing for the add() and edit() calls and flush once before returning to avoid possibly accidentally triggering other constraints.

issue_title: dropout layers leak float64 into graph
issue_body:
When using a dropout layer, the output upcasts to float64, causing every layer after it to be upcast to float64. The problem seems to be caused by the way numpy treats operations that upcast integers. For example:

x = 1 - numpy.float32(0.5)

causes x to have type numpy.float64

This happens to me on numpy version 1.9.0, but I think it happens on other versions as well.

So, here is a code fix:

Line 73 of noise.py - which reads:
retain_prob = 1 - self.p

should be changed to something like this:
if theano.config.floatX == 'float64' :
   retain_prob = np.float64(1 - self.p)
else :
   retain_prob = np.float32(1 - self.p)

issue_title: 'NaN'  when using nesterov momentum and high learning rates
issue_body:
**Introduction**
On August 21, I reported a bug on the lasagne group forum about this.
Basically, when you are using nesterov momentum to train conv nets, if your learning rate starts too high, "nans" are generated to the function loss (training and validation loss), accuracy remains normal. In my case, they start happening near epoch 9.

Obs : The bug seems to happen more often when your dataset is unbalanced or has a lot of values of a single label.

This happens at the most new update of theano and lasagne.

Right now, im using a current version of lasagne that is old but doesnt have that issue : 
"pip install --upgrade --no-deps git+git://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne.git@5a009f98cb479c6c39157027de830a83462dabc0"


Jan.schluete asked me to bisect the code with git to find the guilty commit.
I did it twice to confirm and found it.

_4d4e0b0796634c23ad43889685ee4b428fe30f8a is the first bad commit
commit 4d4e0b0796634c23ad43889685ee4b428fe30f8a
Author: Jan Schlüter <jan.schlueter@    .at>
Date:   Thu Jun 30 13:30:29 2016 +0200

    Have create_param() set the broadcast pattern of created shared variables

:040000 040000 93ce1ab99b4d4bd14d3825a0b143109aa7d234b2 020a514a738f4fd6d29f538fa2d0fdc5af76555b M      lasagne_


The model im using and the learning rate im using are below :  

```
def getModel():
    try:

        input_var = tensor.tensor4('inputs')
        target_var = tensor.fmatrix('targets')

        # Input Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(shape=(None, 1, 7, 7), input_var=input_var)

        # First Convolution Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
        network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

        # Second Convolution Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
        network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

        # Hidden Layers
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=96)
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=32)
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=1, nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.sigmoid)

        return network, input_var, target_var

    except Exception as inst:
        print ("Failure to Build NN !", inst.message, type(inst), (inst.args), (inst))
        sys.exit(1)

def getLearningRate(current_epoch=0, max_epochs=1000):
    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 5) / 100:
        return float32(0.150)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 10) / 100:
        return float32(0.10)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 30) / 100:
        return float32(0.010)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 80) / 100:
        return float32(0.0010)

    if current_epoch <= (max_epochs * 100) / 100:
        return float32(0.00010)
```

Momentum is kept at default.

**Same issue!**


>   If you are not sure whether the problem lies within your code, Theano, or Lasagne, first post on [our mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/lasagne-users).

**The problem is in the commit informed above.**

Please help!

I recreated the bug using the mnist dataset. However, due to the different data im using, i had to transform every y[y>1] = 0 , so it simulates the unbalance of my dataset.

And yes, the bug starts happening either at the initial epochs ( 2 or 3) or before 10 epochs.

It doesnt happen on the version of lasagne i mentioned before : git+git://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne.git@5a009f98cb479c6c39157027de830a83462dabc0

I tested more than 10 times in the bleeding edge version and more than 10 times on the version above.

The script on the zip and below simulates it.
[Main_RebalancedMnist.zip](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/files/573994/Main_RebalancedMnist.zip)

```
#CPATH=~/cuda/include:$CPATH PATH=~/cuda/bin/:$PATH LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/cuda/lib64/:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH THEANO_FLAGS=mode=FAST_RUN,device=gpu1,floatX=float32,optimizer_including=cudnn
# python Main_RebalancedMnist.py
#Using gpu device 1: GeForce GTX TITAN X (CNMeM is enabled with initial size: 95.0% of memory, cuDNN 5105)
#THEANO RC SETTINGS:
#
#[lib]
#cnmem=1

#[dnn]
#enabled=True
from __future__ import print_function

import os
import sys
import time

import lasagne
import numpy as np
import theano
import theano.tensor as T


def float32(k):
    return np.cast['float32'](k)


# ################## Download and prepare the MNIST dataset ##################
def load_dataset():
    # We first define a download function, supporting both Python 2 and 3.
    if sys.version_info[0] == 2:
        from urllib import urlretrieve
    else:
        from urllib.request import urlretrieve

    def download(filename, source='http://yann.lecun.com/exdb/mnist/'):
        print("Downloading %s" % filename)
        urlretrieve(source + filename, filename)

    # We then define functions for loading MNIST images and labels.
    # For convenience, they also download the requested files if needed.
    import gzip

    def load_mnist_images(filename):
        if not os.path.exists(filename):
            download(filename)
        # Read the inputs in Yann LeCun's binary format.
        with gzip.open(filename, 'rb') as f:
            data = np.frombuffer(f.read(), np.uint8, offset=16)
        # The inputs are vectors now, we reshape them to monochrome 2D images,
        # following the shape convention: (examples, channels, rows, columns)
        data = data.reshape(-1, 1, 28, 28)
        # The inputs come as bytes, we convert them to float32 in range [0,1].
        # (Actually to range [0, 255/256], for compatibility to the version
        # provided at http://deeplearning.net/data/mnist/mnist.pkl.gz.)
        return data / np.float32(256)

    def load_mnist_labels(filename):
        if not os.path.exists(filename):
            download(filename)
        # Read the labels in Yann LeCun's binary format.
        with gzip.open(filename, 'rb') as f:
            data = np.frombuffer(f.read(), np.uint8, offset=8)
        # The labels are vectors of integers now, that's exactly what we want.
        return data

    # We can now download and read the training and test set images and labels.
    X_train = load_mnist_images('train-images-idx3-ubyte.gz')
    y_train = load_mnist_labels('train-labels-idx1-ubyte.gz')
    X_test = load_mnist_images('t10k-images-idx3-ubyte.gz')
    y_test = load_mnist_labels('t10k-labels-idx1-ubyte.gz')

    # We reserve the last 10000 training examples for validation.
    X_train, X_val = X_train[:-10000], X_train[-10000:]
    y_train, y_val = y_train[:-10000], y_train[-10000:]

    # We just return all the arrays in order, as expected in main().
    # (It doesn't matter how we do this as long as we can read them again.)
    return X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, X_test, y_test


def build_cnn(input_var=None):
    # Input Layer
    network = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(shape=(None, 1, 28, 28), input_var=input_var)

    # First Convolution Layer
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
    network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

    # Second Convolution Layer
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
    network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
    network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

    # Hidden Layers
    network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=96)
    network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=32)
    network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=1, nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.sigmoid)

    return network


def iterate_minibatches(inputs, targets, batchsize):
    assert len(inputs) == len(targets)

    indices = np.arange(len(inputs))
    np.random.shuffle(indices)

    for start_idx in range(0, len(inputs) - batchsize + 1, batchsize):
        excerpt = indices[start_idx:start_idx + batchsize]
        yield inputs[excerpt], targets[excerpt]


# ############################## Main program ################################
def main():
    num_epochs = 500
    batch_size = 3000
    learning_rate = 0.150
    momentum_rho = 0.9

    seed = 8000
    lasagne.random.set_rng(np.random.RandomState(seed))

    print("\nnum_epochs " + str(num_epochs))
    print("batch_size " + str(batch_size))
    print("learning_rate " + str(learning_rate))
    print("momentum_rho " + str(momentum_rho))
    print("seed " + str(seed))


    # Load the dataset
    print("\nLoading data...")
    X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val, X_test, y_test = load_dataset()

    # REBALANCE DATASET TO 0,1 - SIMULATE A DATASET WITH A LOT MORE 0's THAN 1's.

    y_train.flags.writeable = True
    y_val.flags.writeable = True
    y_test.flags.writeable = True

    def rebalance(k):
        k[k > 1] = 0
        return k

    X_train = X_train.reshape(-1, 1, 28, 28)
    X_val = X_val.reshape(-1, 1, 28, 28)
    X_test = X_test.reshape(-1, 1, 28, 28)

    y_train = y_train.reshape(-1, 1)
    y_test = y_test.reshape(-1, 1)
    y_val = y_val.reshape(-1, 1)

    y_train = rebalance(y_train)
    y_val = rebalance(y_val)
    y_test = rebalance(y_test)

    # Prepare Theano variables for inputs and targets
    input_var = T.tensor4('inputs')
    target_var = T.fmatrix('targets')

    # Create neural network model (depending on first command line parameter)
    print("Building model and compiling functions...")
    network = build_cnn(input_var)

    prediction = lasagne.layers.get_output(network)
    loss = lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(prediction, target_var)
    loss = loss.mean()

    params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(network, trainable=True)
    updates = lasagne.updates.nesterov_momentum(loss, params, learning_rate=float32(learning_rate), momentum=float32(momentum_rho))

    test_prediction = lasagne.layers.get_output(network, deterministic=True)
    test_loss = lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(test_prediction, target_var)
    test_loss = test_loss.mean()

    test_acc = lasagne.objectives.binary_accuracy(test_prediction, target_var).mean()

    train_fn = theano.function([input_var, target_var], loss, updates=updates)

    val_fn = theano.function([input_var, target_var], [test_loss, test_acc])

    # Finally, launch the training loop.
    print("Starting training...")

    for epoch in range(num_epochs):
        # In each epoch, we do a full pass over the training data:
        train_err = 0
        train_batches = 0
        start_time = time.time()
        for batch in iterate_minibatches(X_train, y_train, batch_size):
            inputs, targets = batch
            train_err += train_fn(inputs, targets)
            train_batches += 1

        # And a full pass over the validation data:
        val_err = 0
        val_acc = 0
        val_batches = 0
        for batch in iterate_minibatches(X_val, y_val, batch_size):
            inputs, targets = batch
            err, acc = val_fn(inputs, targets)
            val_err += err
            val_acc += acc
            val_batches += 1

        # Then we print the results for this epoch:
        print("Epoch {} of {} took {:.3f}s".format(epoch + 1, num_epochs, time.time() - start_time))
        print("  training loss:\t\t{:.6f}".format(train_err / train_batches))
        print("  validation loss:\t\t{:.6f}".format(val_err / val_batches))
        print("  validation accuracy:\t\t{:.2f} %".format(val_acc / val_batches * 100))

    # After training, we compute and print the test error:
    test_err = 0
    test_acc = 0
    test_batches = 0
    for batch in iterate_minibatches(X_test, y_test, batch_size):
        inputs, targets = batch
        err, acc = val_fn(inputs, targets)
        test_err += err
        test_acc += acc
        test_batches += 1

    print("Final results:")
    print("  test loss:\t\t\t{:.6f}".format(test_err / test_batches))
    print("  test accuracy:\t\t{:.2f} %".format(test_acc / test_batches * 100))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

What are your plans now that theano is going to be discontinued?

We, the pymc3 devs, have discussed all kinds of options. One of them is taking over theano maintenance (not pushing new features just making sure it doesn't go stale and critical bugs get fixed). Is that anything to talk about?

It should be possible to easily store trained models. There are two ways to do this:

1) store the parameter values. This is typically what I do myself. The actual architecture of the model is not stured, but the definition of the model architecture is just a Python file, and that is where the architecture is 'stored', as far as I'm concerned. So I'd do something like this:
```
l_out = ... # output layer of the network
all_params = nntools.layers.get_all_params(l_out)
all_param_values = [p.get_value() for p in all_params]
store(all_param_values, ...)
```
where `store` is a placeholder for pickling or something to that extent.

To load this model again:
```
all_param_values = load(...)
all_params = nntools.layers.get_all_params(l_out)
for p, v in zip(all_params, all_param_values):
    p.set_value(v)
```

This works because get_all_params always gives you the parameter variables in the same order. We should probably provide two utility functions for this.

2) pickle the entire model, parameters included (i.e. `l_out`). I personally don't do this usually, but it's kind of the natural thing to do in Python and I believe a lot of people will try this and expect it to work. So I think we should support this approach.

This imposes some limitations on what language features we can use. For example, lambdas are not 'picklable', and functions aren't either unless they are defined in the top level of a module (see https://docs.python.org/2/library/pickle.html#what-can-be-pickled-and-unpickled ). So some functional programming patterns cannot be used if we want to keep everything picklable.

That's unfortunate, but I think it is a good idea to keep picklability in mind nevertheless. What does everyone think?

I still think we should support the approach of storing only the parameter values as well, though.

1) In the Creating TimeSeries section, after the ephys_ts line, there is:
    f.add_raw_timeseries(ts, [ep1, ep2])
I think it should be this to match:

@wbwakeman

Subtle bug, but here it goes:

On test_standard_iterator_unbuffered (tests/unit/form_tests/utils_test/test_core_DataChunkIterator), line 57 and 59, we have
dci = DataChunkIterator(data=range(10), buffer_size=1)
self.assertTupleEqual(dci.max_shape, (None,))

I claim it should read:
self.assertTupleEqual(dci.max_shape, (10,))

This is because in the constructor of DataChunkIterator we have:

```        if self.max_shape is None:
            # If the self.data object identifies it shape then use it
            if hasattr(self.data,  "shape"):
                self.max_shape = self.data.shape
                # Avoid the special case of scalar values by making them into a 1D numpy array
                if len(self.max_shape) == 0:
                    self.data = np.asarray([self.data, ])
                    self.max_shape = self.data.shape
                    self.__data_iter = iter(self.data)
            # Try to get an accurate idea of max_shape for other Python datastructures if possible.
            # Don't just callget_shape for a generator as that would potentially trigger loading of all the data
            elif isinstance(self.data, list) or isinstance(self.data, tuple):
                self.max_shape = ShapeValidator.get_data_shape(self.data)```


Initially, max_shape is None will be true, and data will not have attribut.  So the flow control will pass to line ```elif isinstance(self.data, list) or isinstance(self.data, tuple):```

However, this will not evaluate to true in python3, because data is type "range" (in python 2, it is type list, so the line will evaluate True, and max_shape would be set to 10).  

In python3 max_shape will not be set, so the test will pass, but I think this is incorrect behavior for the test.  I think ```elif isinstance(self.data, list) or isinstance(self.data, tuple):``` should be replaced by ```elif isinstance(self.data, (list, tuple, range)):``` and tests or other code should be adjusted accordingly.  This is a short job, but one prone to mistakes, and since it involves rewriting test behavior, I vote for @ajtritt to make the modifications if he agrees.

issue_title: Add docs for predicates to http://data8.org/datascience/
Export to .XLSX an error occurred in tablib "TypeError: cell() missing 1 required positional argument: 'column'"
issue_body:
**When export to .xlsx format, an error occurred in tablib, throw an error message below:**

File "............/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tablib/formats/_xlsx.py", line 122, in dset_sheet
    cell = ws.cell('%s%s' % (col_idx, row_number))
TypeError: cell() missing 1 required positional argument: 'column'

**I googled it seems like an version compatibility problem with tablib.**

It says: 
> You have a newer openpyxl version installed with your 3.6 installation compared to your 3.5 setup.

> The version you have installed with 3.6 has removed the deprecated coordinate parameter from worksheet.cell() method and made the row and column mandatory arguments. This is part of version 2.5.0b1, released on 2018-01-19 (two weeks ago):

> Major Changes

> worksheet.cell() no longer accepts a coordinate parameter. The syntax is now ws.cell(row, column, value=None)
> The tablib library has not yet adjusted to this change. The code should just pass in the column and row numbers directly:

> cell = ws.cell(row=row_number, column=col_idx)
> Using keyword arguments would ensure compatibility all the way back to 1.1.0 (the release that added support for the column and row parameters, released in 2010).

> In the meantime, you can downgrade your openpyxl installation to version 2.4.9, the last release without those changes.


So I think we should fix this version compatibility problem somehow. 
Thank you guys.

issue_title: Please fix asymmetry in rs::context notifications about devices being added / removed
issue_body:
* Before opening a new issue, we wanted to provide you with some useful suggestions (Click "Preview" above for a better view):

    * Consider checking out SDK [examples](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/examples#sample-code-for-intel-realsense-cameras).
    * Have you looked in our [documentations](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/tree/master/doc#useful-links)?
    * Is you question a [frequently asked one](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/Troubleshooting-Q%26A)?
    * Try [searching our GitHub Issues](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue) (open and closed) for a similar issue.

* All users are welcomed to report bugs, ask questions, suggest or request enhancements and generally feel free to open new issue, even if they haven't followed any of the suggestions above :)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D400 series (D400, D415, D435) | 
| Firmware Version                   | N/A | 
| Operating System & Version |   All  | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |  All                                         | 
| Platform                                 | All  |
| SDK Version                            |  Master as of 719b0b95cdfd6a5c9a3a07e34bea48fce5f35d2a                          | 
| Language                            |  C++, Python                         | 
| Segment			|  All                          | 

### Issue Description
The C++ (and, consequently, python API which is what I actually care about) for notifications when devices are added / removed is asymmetric. The events expose a list of newly added devices but force you to query for the removal of specific devices. I would like these to be made symmetric in the following ways:

1. Add a method like `bool was_added(const rs2::device& dev) const` to see if a specific device has been re-added (e.g. after issuing a software reset to the device). The signature may need to change (e.g. instead of assuming you have a live device handle, support doing this by serial number maybe since the handles must have potentially been invalidated by the reset event)

2. Add a method like `device_list get_removed_devices() const`

3. Update all the bindings to reflect these changes please :-)

issue_title: Params starting with 'h' are not saved
issue_body:
The problem is that, internally, netpyne stores the NEURON objects in fields starting with ‘h’. Then, before saving to file, it removes these objects (makes them null) to avoid errors. I hadn’t realized the (pretty obvious!) case that parameters starting with an ‘h’ won’t be saved.

To fix, use 'hObj' for all NEURON objects and replace only those with null

issue_title: Assert that two publishes do not modify the RPM repodata
issue_body:
Test setup:
- Create a repo and sync+publish it (ie: zoo)

The test:
- Get the contents of repodata from the published repo
- publish zoo a second time
- Get the contents of repodata from the published repo now

Assert that all the metadata files and their filenames are identical.

If Pulp is publishing a second time, when there is nothing to do, it should do nothing. In the past a republish caused metadata to get republished in all cases which changes the checksum included in the filename. That was undesirable since the repodata itself didn't change the checksums shouldn't change.

This was believed to already be fixed so there isn't an open issue. For now this guarantee is only expected to be on RPM content, but I think it makes sense for all content types.

### Description of Issue/Question
Junos has a concept of physical and logical interfaces.
Physical: xe-0/0/0
Logical xe-0/0/0.0

In practice this means xe-0/0/0 will hold physical properties of a port e.g. speed. xe-0/0/0.0 will hold logical properties such as IP addresses etc.

AFAICT pybind11 supports Eigen maps ('views') as return values and not as arguments (I get a compilation error about a delete function and in a nearby comment it says that maps are not supported as arguments but without an explanation as to why) and I don't see why this should be the case. In the documentation you recommend the use of the Eigen::Ref template. However Refs are slightly heavier and at the same time less versatile (in certain contexts I care about, like adapting/viewing memory of arbitrary yet 'compatible' objects as Eigen types, e.g. OpenCV Mats) than Maps and my codebase is already based on (that is to say littered with:) Maps so changing all that is not a (happy) option.
Could you add this support?

Hi,

I am long time-user of boost.python, and I just started using pybind11. I am already amazed how familiar, yet more comfortable it is. Great work!

The only thing I am missing so far, is support for the None type on the C++ side. In boost.python, it is represented by a default constructed boost::python::object. It is useful to have as a default for a function argument. When I try to use a pybind11::object() as the default argument for a pybind11::arg in this way, I get an error message.

ImportError: arg(): could not convert default keyword argument into a Python object (type not registered yet?). Compile in debug mode for more information.

issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-nsone` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_nsone.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

#### Use Case / Scenario:

Suppose Joe User has set up his own web site and obtained a certificate from letsencrypt.   At some point down the road, the site gets pwned by hackers.  The first thing Joe is going to do is take down the site.  He's going to preserve the disk for forensic analysis.  It may take him a while to obtain new hardware and configure a replacement site.  Meanwhile, the bad guys are doing who-knows-what with his certificate.  Because his site is down, Joe has no way of revoking the certificate, according to the instructions given at:  https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/technology/#certificate-issuance-and-revocation

#### Desired Behavior / Suggested Solution:

Standard good practice in the security business suggests that whenever any certificate is issued by letsencrypt, a ***revocation request*** should be issued also.  It should be contained in a file, along with instructions on how to use it.

> -----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE-----
>  * Put this file on a thumb drive and store it in your first-aid kit, along with bandages and other things that you keep on hand but hope never to need.  (Don't leave the only copy on your server, because you might need it sometime when the server is down.)
>  * If you ever need to revoke the associated certificate, email this file to <revoke@letsencrypt.org> or upload it via the form at http://letsencrypt.org/revoke/.
>  * This revocation request is associated with your certificate www.xxx.yyy.pem issued on dd-mmm-yyyy.

#### Details:

The revocation request contains a human-readable but structured payload.  It is PGP-signed by letsencrypt.org, so we know it's authentic and nobody has any doubt about what it means.  For practical purposes the only thing that is needed is the serial number of the certificate to be revoked, but additional details improve usability and instill user confidence.  Perhaps something like this:
```
Certificate to be revoked:
    Data:
        Serial Number:
            01:4f:1d:54:f3:d7:cc:0f:6e:.....
    Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption
        Validity
            Not Before: Nov  5 20:05:00 2015 GMT
            Not After : Feb  3 20:05:00 2016 GMT
        Subject: CN=www.example.com
        X509v3 extensions:
            X509v3 Subject Alternative Name: 
                DNS:example.com, DNS:www.example.com
-----END PGP SIGNED MESSAGE-----
-----BEGIN PGP SIGNATURE-----
whatever
-----END PGP SIGNATURE-----
```

#### Also:

It should go without saying that the documentation should be updated to highlight this feature and explain how to use it.

issue_title: Add + fix up missing API docs
issue_labels: [{'id': 277080619, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20code%20health', 'name': 'area: code health', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 245387015, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20documentation', 'name': 'area: documentation', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 149596829, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/Good%20Volunteer%20Task', 'name': 'Good Volunteer Task', 'color': 'c61aff', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
If you look at the sidebar on https://certbot.eff.org/docs/api.html, you'll see the modules which have documentation available (e.g. `certbot.crypto_util`). Several of our modules don't appear (e.g. `certbot.cert_manager`), as they don't have entries in `docs/`.

There are two main parts to this issue:
1. Find modules that are missing from `docs/` and add them in. See the existing examples to figure out how to do this.
2. Review the Sphinx-style documentation in the modules you are going to add (or ones that are already included), and add any extra information if you can. Sometimes we didn't have time to include anything beyond a short comment explaining what the function does, but other kinds of documentation can be added (e.g. parameter types, return types, errors raised, etc ... )

See here for more info on how to work with our documentation: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#updating-the-documentation

This task would be a great way for a first-time contributor to be introduced to certbot's codebase!

issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
Ubuntu 16.04

Git - commit `31805c5`

```bash

    self._cleanup_challenges(active_achalls)

    env = self.env.pop(achall.domain)
KeyError: u'xenial.alberon.co.uk'
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It crashed.

This only seems to happen when using `--manual-cleanup-hook` *and* including both `xenial.alberon.co.uk` and `*.xenial.alberon.co.uk`.

I think `self.env.pop(achall.domain)` gets called twice, both times with `achall.domain = 'xenial.alberon.co.uk'`, and the second time it's already been removed. I would guess the key needs to be changed to something else now that one domain may require two separate challenges.

letsencrypt renew --email "alpha_one_x86@first-world.info" --agree-tos --rsa-key-size 4096
But the problem it's, I have 31 sub domain into /etc/letsencrypt/live/, I don't wish change my structure. It's at least minutes of down due to serial pooling. Can you speed up doing this query in parallel/async to query multiple certificate at times, and prevent round-trip delay time problem?

Cheers,

issue_title: Post-Hook not executed and log has no timestamps
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm using a cron job to automatically run the renewal (with certbot 0.8.1) using:
`30 2 * * 1 certbot renew --post-hook "systemctl reload nginx" >> /var/log/letsencrypt/renew-cronjob.log`

First of all: It seems like the post-hook has not been executed. I set a reminder to take a look at the website 2 days before the certificate expires and I'm glad I did this! My browser showed that the certificate expires in 2 days. So I went to the `/etc/letsencrypt/live/website.com` folder and the timestamp of the files were quite new, so after running `systemctl nginx reload` manually, my browser showed the new certificate with the new expiration date.

Second:
Looking at the logs trying to determine if there was an error message I couldn't tell if and when the certificate has been updated, since there are no timestamps in the log... 

The log just shows this repeatedly:
```
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/website.com.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/subdomain.website.com.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/mail.website.com.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following certs are not due for renewal yet:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/website.com/fullchain.pem (skipped)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/subdomain.website.com/fullchain.pem (skipped)
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/mail.website.com/fullchain.pem (skipped)
No renewals were attempted.
```

Sadly the letsencrypt.log files are already overwritten/deleted from the time where the certificate has been renewed and the error should have popped up. But I'll take a look at the logs in 2 months.

Have you experienced the issue with the post-hook not being executed before? And is there anything I might have misconfigured?

Related to https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/1

I'm having trouble figuring out an appropriate pattern for including CORS support. My use-case is that I am connecting a React app to an ASGI backend that uses Starlette's routing and response classes, but enabling CORS support would require altering the response class headers which cannot be accomplished simply by wrapping the ASGI app at this point.

What should CORS support look like in Starlette?

An example of my use-case can be found [here](https://github.com/erm/apistar-react/blob/master/app.py), it uses APIStar and a CORS extension to achieve what I want to do with Starlette as well. Below is my work-in-progress attempt at an ASGI CORS middleware that I would ultimately like to work into Starlette somehow.


```python3
from starlette import PlainTextResponse
from starlette.datastructures import Headers

ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_ORIGIN = b"Access-Control-Allow-Origin"
ACCESS_CONTROL_EXPOSE_HEADERS = b"Access-Control-Expose-Headers"
ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS = b"Access-Control-Allow-Credentials"
ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_HEADERS = b"Access-Control-Allow-Headers"
ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_METHODS = b"Access-Control-Allow-Methods"
ACCESS_CONTROL_MAX_AGE = b"Access-Control-Max-Age"
ACCESS_CONTROL_REQUEST_METHOD = b"Access-Control-Request-Method"
ACCESS_CONTROL_REQUEST_HEADERS = b"Access-Control-Request-Headers"


DEFAULT_OPTIONS = {
    "allow_origin": [b"*"],
    "allow_credentials": False,
    "allow_headers": [b"*"],
    "allow_methods": [
        b"GET",
        b"HEAD",
        b"POST",
        b"OPTIONS",
        b"PUT",
        b"PATCH",
        b"DELETE",
    ],
    "expose_headers": [b""],
    "max_age": b"86400",
}


class CORSMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app, options=None):
        self.app = app
        self.options = options or DEFAULT_OPTIONS

    def __call__(self, scope):
        headers = Headers(scope.get("headers", []))
        origin = headers.get("origin", None)
        if origin is None:
            return self.app(scope)

        allow_origin = self.options["allow_origin"]
        if origin not in allow_origin and b"*" not in allow_origin:
            return PlainTextResponse("Origin not allowed", status_code=400)

        if scope["method"] == "OPTIONS":
            method = headers.get(ACCESS_CONTROL_REQUEST_METHOD, None)
            if method is None:
                return PlainTextResponse(
                    "Access-Control-Request-Method header missing", status_code=400
                )

        cors_headers = []
        if origin != "*" and len(allow_origin) > 1:  # todo double-check this
            cors_headers.append((b"vary", b"origin"))

        cors_allow_origin = b", ".join(self.options["allow_origin"])
        cors_allow_credentials = self.options["allow_credentials"]
        cors_allow_headers = b", ".join(self.options["allow_headers"])
        cors_allow_methods = b", ".join(self.options["allow_methods"])
        cors_expose_headers = b", ".join(self.options["expose_headers"])
        cors_max_age = self.options["max_age"]

        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_ORIGIN, cors_allow_origin))
        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_METHODS, cors_allow_methods))
        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_HEADERS, cors_allow_headers))
        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS, cors_allow_credentials))
        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_EXPOSE_HEADERS, cors_expose_headers))
        cors_headers.append((ACCESS_CONTROL_MAX_AGE, cors_max_age))

        scope["headers"].extend(cors_headers)
        print(scope["headers"])

        return self.app(scope)
```

issue_title: Unexpected behavior of "check" flag in addCons
issue_body:
Dear PySCIPOpt Team,

I have been running some experiments where I need to modify the "check" flag, and I have been observing some strange behavior. I am attaching a Python script that evidences this.

In this example, I create a minimization problem whose optimal value is -17. If I add some redundant non-linear constraints with "check=True" then the value stays at -17 as expected. However, if I set "check=False" in one of these redundant constraints, the value jumps to 0, which shouldn't happen, as SCIP should be checking for fewer constraints for feasibility.

I hope you can help me with this, and I apologize if the issue is something simple I'm not seeing.

Best,
Gonzalo.

issue_title: Can't start Raiden without internet connection
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Upon start Raiden tries to gain external connectivity via UPNP or STUN. If no internet connection is available both methods fail and Raiden crashes with `STUNUnavailableException`.

In normal use this would only be a cosmetic issue (exception instead of a more user friendly error messge) since the public chain isn't accessible w/o internet anyway. However usage scenarios in isolated networks on a private chain are certainly imaginable and should be supported.

## Solution

Don't crash if both UPNP and STUN are unavailable but instead print a warning and fall back to using the local ip / port.

## Tasklist
- [x] Implement `--nat` option (see comment below)
- [x] Warn user about likely connection problems if no nat traversal is possible
- [x] Add changelog

issue_title: Where to limit the balance proof's `transferred_amount` - overflow at settlement
issue_body:
A very big `transferred_amount` can cause an overflow when calculating the settlement amounts. this will cause the participant's tokens to be sent to the other participant.

The issue is where to set a limit to `transferred_amount`, which is going to be relative to the `deposit`.
My proposal is to only set this limit in the Raiden and not in the smart contract.

Reasons:
- `deposit` value can be very different per token
- Raiden client has access to all variables needed to set this limit dynamically
- Raiden client is the official client to be used. Using anything else of course poses a risk

Discussions:
https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/pull/114#discussion_r194008259

issue_title: Order for first()/last() is reversed when getting polymorphic objects.
issue_body:
`.last()` is meant to return the last object from the queryset, ie the one with the highest PK by default, but it's returning the first instead.

Same goes for `.first()` which returns the last.

This is not the case when dealing with non polymorphic objects.

issue_title: Interface for job submission on clusters
issue_body:
With the completion of #414 (remote execution of tasks), and planned wait feature ( #436) of the sos core, we will need to find a good way to submit jobs on clusters.

I would propose that we make use of the `CONFIG['hosts'][host]` feature to define behavior for each cluster. Something like:

```
hosts:
   cluster:
        address:    address of head node
        path_map:  how to map files
        shared:        shared filesystem, not likely to have one
        send_cmd:  how to send files to cluster, default value should work
        receive_cmd: how to receive files from cluster, default value should work
        execution_cmd:  THIS WOULD NEED SOME THOUGHT
        wait:                False
        status_cmd:      Status checking command
```

Basically, 

1. option `wait` tells SoS that the job would be in waiting status after `sos execute task` returns, which triggers command `sos execute task --no-wait`.
2. option `execution_cmd`. Instead of `ssh head_node sos execute task`, it would be something like `ssh head_node qsub sos execute task`
3. option `status_cmd` would be a command to check status, something like `ssh head_node sos execute task --check-status`

There are two missing pieces here:

1. SoS does not know how to pass resource options to PBS. Something like

  ```
  job_template:  "#PBS -wall ${_runtime["wall"]}
  ```

  might do.

2. SoS does not know how to check the status of the job. Supposedly the `qsub` command would return some ID number but there is no general way to perform `--check-status`.

This all looks troublesome, but at the end of the day (month, year?), we should be able to do

```
[10]
task: on_host='cluster', to_host, from_host

```

and submit tasks to the cluster in Jupyter notebook, or from command line using

```
sos run script --no-wait
```

to submit all the jobs without waiting for their completion, or

```
sos run script --no-wait
```

again to check the status (sos would try to submit jobs but found that they are still running), and perform latter steps if some jobs are completed, and

```
sos run script
```

to wait for the completion of jobs and continue the workflow after the jobs are completed. Interruption of this command should not affect the execution of remote jobs.

issue_title: Parameter for older and newer than
issue_body:
Such a parameter can be used in `sos remove` and `%tasks` to select files, signatures, or tasks that are older or newer than specified time, but it is hard enough to differentiate between creation and modified time, older and newer, and minutes, hours, days etc. 

Note that command `find` has a bunch of such parameters and we certain do not want all of them.

```
-amin, -atime, -cmin, -ctime, -mmin, -mtime
```

Perhaps we can do something like

```
--age +1d 
--age -1d
--age +5m
```
for modified more than 1 day, less than 1day, and more than 5 minutes ago.

User should be able to add strings to a Trie after instantiation
issue_body:
As a user, I want to create an instance of `Trie` and add words to it one-at-a-time, so that I can use a `Trie` in a streaming environment (in which strings arrive on-the-fly). For example,

```python
>>> from marise import Trie
>>> trie = Trie()
>>> trie.add(u'key1')
>>> trie.add(u'key12')
>>> u'key1' in trie
True
>>> u'key12' in trie
True
>>> u'key2' in trie
False
```

This also makes `Trie` behave more like a `set` of strings.

issue_title: Upload file to a specific folder in Canvas
issue_body:
Hi,
Right now I can't seem to get a file named "Homework_11.pdf" uploaded to the "Homeworks/" folder under my course. The following example works:
```python
fh = open('path/to/Homework_11.pdf')
canvas = Canvas(API_URL, API_KEY)
course_id = 1113938
course = canvas.get_course(course_id)
course.upload(fh)
```
However, it gets uploaded to a new folder named "unfiled".

I sniffed the Canvas website to see what it does versus the canvaslmsapi.Uploader class.
It does the following:
`POST https://auburn.instructure.com/api/v1/folders/{folder_id}/files`

data submitted:
```
name: HW11CSamp.pdf
size: 231844
content_type: application/pdf
on_duplicate: rename
parent_folder_id: 5816809
no_redirect: true
_method: POST
authenticity_token:
```
So at this point it seems to follow the standard pattern of returning info on where to upload the file that the Uploader class handles. I am going to add a canvasapi.folder.Folder.upload() method to do this. Would be cool to pass a Folder object into class.upload(fh, folder=canvasapi.folder.Folder) to do this as well.

Let me know and I can send you guys what I got. Thank you so much for this library. It is a lifesaver!!!

issue_title: Questions on specific details of the GP algorithm
issue_body:
Currently i am going to the literature on TPOT, and there are some unclarities in what i read w.r.t. what i find in the code. The main question i have here is a about the selection of individuals that proceed to the next generation. In the code i see that new population is selected from both the old population and the offspring. In the [paper](http://proceedings.mlr.press/v64/olson_tpot_2016.pdf) i found, if i understand correctly it is said that the selection is of the new population is done only from the offspring.

It would be really helpful if you could shed some light on these choices. Are these developments in the recent versions? Are there more major changes in the inner workings of TPOT w.r.t. what is written in the latest papers on TPOT? Thanks a lot in advance :) .

issue_title: AssertionError:  Found no NVIDIA driver on your system.
issue_body:
Hey, I'm working on machine with no GPU, trying to load one of the Amazon reviews sentiment datasets into torchtext. With the following MCVE 

```python
filepath = './data/reviews_Amazon_Instant_Video_5.json'
TEXT = data.Field(sequential=True, lower=True)

dataset = data.TabularDataset(
    path=filepath, format='json',
    fields={'reviewText': ('text', TEXT),
            'overall': ('labels', data.Field(sequential=False, use_vocab=False, tensor_type=torch.DoubleTensor))})

TEXT.build_vocab(dataset)
iterator = data.Iterator(dataset, 128, shuffle=True)

next(iter(iterator))
```

I get 

```
AssertionError: 
Found no NVIDIA driver on your system. Please check that you
have an NVIDIA GPU and installed a driver from
http://www.nvidia.com/Download/index.aspx
```

I feel like the default assumption should be to use the CPU, right? Since I didn't specify a device anywhere.

I can fix this by explicitly setting the device to `-1` in the `Iterator`:
```python
iterator = data.Iterator(dataset, 128, device=-1, shuffle=True)
```

Is this intended behavior?
